title: MGMT 3130 Rubric
description: Comprehensive rubric for analyzing student essays on planning fallacy

questions.title: Explain the concept of planning fallacy
questions.description: Student should demonstrate understanding of planning fallacy definition and causes

scoringCriteria.description: Excellent understanding with clear definition and multiple causes identified
Good understanding with adequate definition and some causes identified
Basic understanding with simple definition
Poor or no understanding demonstrated

scoreLevels.description: Excellent understanding with clear definition and multiple causes identified
Good understanding with adequate definition and some causes identified
Basic understanding with simple definition
Poor or no understanding demonstrated

questions.label: 1a

questions.title: Explain psychological factors related to planning fallacy
questions.description: Student should identify and explain psychological factors contributing to planning fallacy

scoringCriteria.description: Comprehensive explanation of multiple psychological factors with examples
Good explanation of some psychological factors
Basic mention of psychological factors
Little to no explanation of psychological factors

scoreLevels.description: Comprehensive explanation of multiple psychological factors with examples
Good explanation of some psychological factors
Basic mention of psychological factors
Little to no explanation of psychological factors

questions.label: 1b

questions.title: Write a reflection report
questions.description:
WHAT? What have you learned through this seminar? What stands out to you, feels new to you, excites you, or challenges you?

WHY? Why does this learning matter? Why does it stand out to you? Why is it important - to you personally or in the bigger picture?

HOW? What will you do from here? How will you take this learning with you? Does this learning change your perspective, your career goals, or your interests?

scoreLevels.description: Excellent: The language is clear and expressive. The reader can create a mental picture of the situation being described. Abstract concepts are explained accurately. The reflection shows tremendous thought and effort. The learning experience being reflected upon is relevant and meaningful

Pass: Minor, infrequent lapses in clarity. Abstract concepts are explained fairly accurately. The reflections show some thought and effort. Student makes attempts to demonstrate relevance, but the relevance is unclear in reference to learning goals.

Fail: There are frequent lapses in clarity. Concepts are either not discussed or are presented inaccurately. The reflections show poor thought and effort. Most of the reflection is irrelevant to student and/or learning goals.

The reflection moves beyond simple description of the experience to an analysis of how the experience contributed to student understanding of self, others, and/or other concepts. It demonstrates ability of the student to question their own biases, stereotypes, preconceptions, and/or assumptions and define new modes of thinking as a result

The reflection demonstrates student attempts to analyze the experience to understanding of self, but analysis lacks depth. It demonstrates ability of the student to question their own biases, stereotypes, preconceptions. New modes of thinking are not evident.

Student makes attempts at applying the learning experience to understanding of self, others, and/or seminar concepts but fails to demonstrate depth of analysis. There is some attempt at self-criticism, but the self-reflection fails to demonstrate a new awareness of personal biases, etc.
Number of words greater than 200
Number of words less than 200

title: COMP4900 Seminar Reflection Report
description: This rubric assesses the quality of the reflection report based on clarity, analysis, and length.

questions.title: Write a reflection report on the seminar.
questions.description: Students should address their learning experiences, the significance of these learnings, and how they plan to apply this knowledge moving forward.

scoringCriteria.description: Demonstrates deep understanding and insightful reflection.
Shows basic understanding and some reflection.
Lacks understanding and reflection.

scoreLevels.description: The language is clear and expressive. The reader can create a mental picture of the situation being described. Abstract concepts are explained accurately. The reflection shows tremendous thought and effort.
Minor, infrequent lapses in clarity. Abstract concepts are explained fairly accurately. The reflections show some thought and effort.
There are frequent lapses in clarity. Concepts are either not discussed or are presented inaccurately. The reflections show poor thought and effort.
The reflection moves beyond simple description to an analysis of how the experience contributed to student understanding of self, others, and/or other concepts.
The reflection demonstrates student attempts to analyze the experience to understanding of self, but analysis lacks depth.
Student makes attempts at applying the learning experience to understanding of self, others, and/or seminar concepts but fails to demonstrate depth of analysis.
Number of words over 200.
Number of words over 200.
Number of words less than 200.

title: COMP 1023 Midterm Exam Rubric
description: This rubric assesses students' understanding and application of programming concepts in Python as demonstrated through various problem-solving tasks.

questions.title: True/False Questions
questions.description: Students must answer a series of true/false questions accurately.

questions.title: 2a Branching and Looping Statements
questions.description: Students must demonstrate their understanding of branching and looping statements through coding tasks.

scoringCriteria.description: Score reflects the accuracy of the total value and reasoning for termination.

scoreLevels.description: 1 point for each correct total value

questions.title: 2b

scoringCriteria.description: Score reflects the correctness and completeness of the functions implemented.

scoreLevels.description: absolute 1.5 points for the correct return value, else 0
absolute 1.5 points for the correct reason for termination, else 0

questions.modelAnswer: The value that the function returns: 162. Reason for the termination: run out of items naturally

questions.title: 3a(i)

scoringCriteria.description: Score reflects the functionality and correctness of the mini store system.

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for checking divisibility using the modulus operator (%) without loops.
0.5 points for correctly returning a boolean value.

questions.modelAnswer: def is_divisible(n: int, k: int)-> bool: return n % k == 0

questions.title: 3a(ii)

scoringCriteria.description: Score reflects the correctness and functionality of the number guessing game.

criteria.name: using the is_divisible function correctly from part (a)(i).

scoreLevels.description: using the is_divisible function correctly from part (a)(i).

criteria.name: breaking the loop early when the divisor is found

scoreLevels.description: 1 point for breaking the loop early when the divisor is found
running the loop from 2 to at least √n (it is acceptable if the loop runs from 2 to n)

1 point for correctly handling the edge case (i.e., n==2). If the loop iterates from 2 to n, it is acceptable not to include explicit edge-case handling.– If the loop starts from 0 (i.e., for divisor in n), the edge cases divisor == 0, divisor == 1 should be handled.– If the loop runs beyond n, the edge case divisor == n should be handled.

questions.title: 3a(iii)

criteria.name: using the is_prime function from part (a)(ii).

scoreLevels.description: 1.5 points for using the is_prime function from part (a)(ii).

criteria.name: running the loop from 2 to n+1

scoreLevels.description: 1.5 points for running the loop from 2 to n+1
1.5 points for correctly counting the primes
1.5 points for correctly returning the counted value.

questions.title: 3b
questions.description: Deduct 0.25 points for each syntax error. 0 point for hardcoding.  Additional functions not allowed

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for n = len(lst)
2 points for initializing a new n×n 2D list.

criteria.name: having two loops that run from 0 to n

scoreLevels.description: 1.5 points for having two loops that run from 0 to n
2 points for the value assignment
correctly returning the rotated 2D list.
No point with only the return statement without the correct variable.)

questions.title: 4a Mini Store System
questions.modelAnswer:
def print_balance(balance: int)-> None:
print("Your balance is $" + str(balance))

criteria.name: defining the function with the name print_balance

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for correctly defining the function with the name print_balance.
0.5 points for correctly defining the function parameter balance.
0.5 points for correctly printing “Your balance is $”.
0.5 points for correctly printing balance.

questions.title: 4b

criteria.name: correctly defining the function with the name print_bag.

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for correctly defining the function with the name print_bag.

criteria.name: correctly defining the function parameter bag.

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for correctly defining the function parameter bag.
1 point for correctly printing Your bag contains:.

criteria.name: correctly using a loop to print items.

scoreLevels.description: 2 points for correctly using a loop to print items.

criteria.name: correctly printing the statement and output format

scoreLevels.description: 2 points for correctly printing the statement and output format

questions.title: 4c

criteria.name: correctly defining the function with the name put_in_bag.

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for correctly defining the function with the name put_in_bag.

criteria.name: correctly defining the function parameters bag, item, amount.

scoreLevels.description: 1 point for correctly defining the function parameters bag, item, amount.

criteria.name: correctly using a loop to add multiple items.

scoreLevels.description: 2 points for correctly using a loop to add multiple items.
0.5 points for correctly returning the updated bag.

questions.title: 4d

criteria.name: correctly defining the function with the name purchase_item

scoreLevels.description: 0.5 points for correctly defining the function with the name purchase_item.

criteria.name: defining the function parameters balance, item, price, amount

scoreLevels.description: 1.5points for correctly defining the function parameters balance, item, price, amount

criteria.name: correctly handling the default parameter value, i.e., amount = 1.

scoreLevels.description: 1 point for correctly handling the default parameter value, i.e., amount = 1.

criteria.name: correctly implementing the balance deduction logic.

scoreLevels.description: 2.5 points for correctly implementing the balance deduction logic.
2.5 points for correctly returning the value (tuple structure).

title: Sosc 3900 Individual Paper Grading Rubrics
description: This rubric assesses the quality of the individual paper submitted for the course SOSC3900 Abnormal Psychology.

questions.title: Address the following questions using your own words with proper APA citation: Psychologists normally adopt the 'three Ds' (i.e., deviance, dysfunction, and distress) to define abnormality. Which ONE of the 'Ds' do you think a better dimension to define abnormal behaviors? Illustrate your stance with a specific psychological disorder.
questions.description: Students should demonstrate their understanding of the three Ds of abnormality and relate one of them to a specific psychological disorder covered in the course.

scoringCriteria.description: This score level indicates the overall quality of the paper based on the combined evaluation of all criteria.

scoreLevels.description: Demonstrated an outstanding understanding about the three 'Ds' to define abnormality. Elaborated very clearly how the specific psychological disorder is linked with one of the Ds. Provided very insightful and unique discussions on the issue with literature.

Demonstrated a good understanding about the three 'Ds' to define abnormality. Elaborated clearly how the specific psychological disorder is linked with one of the Ds. Provided insightful discussions on the issue with literature.

Demonstrated satisfactory understanding about the three 'Ds' to define abnormality. Elaborated how the specific psychological disorder is linked with one of the Ds, but with some confusing points. Provided appropriate discussions on the issue, but did not support with literature.

Demonstrated mere understanding about the three 'Ds' to define abnormality. Elaborated fairly how the specific psychological disorder is linked with one of the Ds, but with a lot of confusing points. Provided fair discussions on the issue without literature support.

Demonstrated poor understanding about the three 'Ds' to define abnormality. Did not elaborate fairly how the specific psychological disorder is linked with one of the Ds and with a lot of confusing points. Did not provide discussions on the issue.
Very smooth, coherent and well-organized logic of presentation. The writing style follows APA citation and referencing very appropriately.
A coherent and organized logic of presentation. The writing style in general follows APA citation and referencing.
A logic of presentation, but with flaws in coherency of arguments. The writing style in general follows APA citation and referencing.
An unorganized logic of presentation, and with a lot of flaws in coherency of arguments. The writing style did not follow APA citation and referencing.
A poor logic of presentation with incoherency of arguments. The writing style did not follow APA citation and referencing at all.

questions.title: Explain the psychology factor of planning fallacy

scoreLevels.description: Demonstrates a comprehensive understanding of the planning fallacy, including its psychological underpinnings, such as optimism bias and cognitive dissonance. Provides detailed examples and integrates relevant theories, such as the role of temporal discounting and the impact of past experiences on future predictions.

Shows a good understanding of the planning fallacy, discussing key psychological factors like optimism bias. Provides some examples but may lack depth in explaining the underlying theories or fail to connect them effectively to the concept of planning fallacy.

Demonstrates a basic understanding of the planning fallacy, mentioning some psychological factors but lacking clarity or depth. Examples may be vague or not directly related to the concept, and there is minimal reference to relevant theories.

Shows little to no understanding of the planning fallacy. Fails to identify key psychological factors or provide relevant examples. The response may be off-topic or demonstrate significant misconceptions about the concept.
